critic_raw:
This is a well-constructed methodology with several genuine strengths: chronological splitting, preprocessing fit on training only, explicit exclusion of post-receipt features, a relevant operational baseline, and honest scope limitation at the end. The critique below focuses on structural issues that could undermine the validity of the core claim, not implementation details.

**Issue 1: The negative class construction conflates absence-of-evidence with evidence-of-absence, and the 21-day window is asymmetrically applied.**

The specific claim: messages with no malicious disposition after 21 days and no later incident linkage are clean negatives.

Why this might be wrong: Enterprise phishing campaigns frequently have a delayed-harm pattern — credential harvesting that isn't detected until a breach investigation weeks or months later, or silent malware dropper links that are only confirmed malicious when C2 traffic is observed. The 21-day window is measured from receipt, but incident-response confirmation of a positive can come much later than 21 days. This creates an asymmetric labeling rule: positives can be confirmed at any time (including after month 6), but negatives are confirmed at 21 days. If the training period (months 1–4) has had more time for incident retroactive confirmation than month 6 (which may only have had 21–30 days of observation when the experiment is run), the negative class in the test set will be contaminated with future positives at a higher rate than the training negative class. This inflates apparent generalization because the test false-negative rate is understated.

What would constitute evidence: Compare the retroactive-confirmation rate of positives by their receipt month vs. their confirmation date. If a non-trivial fraction of positives from months 1–3 were confirmed more than 21 days after receipt, the negative class in month 6 is suspect. A sensitivity analysis labeling only messages confirmed within 21 days as positives (symmetric cutoff) would bound this effect.

**Issue 2: Deduplication policy creates a train/validation/test asymmetry that may inflate evaluation on campaigns that began in training.**

The specific claim: deduplication within splits removes parser artifacts, while cross-month recurrence is preserved as deployment reality.

Why this might be wrong: Email campaigns commonly reuse not just structural templates but near-identical body and subject text across days and weeks. If a campaign runs from month 3 through month 6, its template was seen in training, near-duplicates were deduplicated within months 3–4 but the same templates appear in month 5–6 evaluation. The model effectively has memorized campaign-specific text patterns and is rewarded for recognizing them in evaluation. The ablation that removes raw text from the transformer pathway would reveal this only if the GBT baseline doesn't also pick up the same campaign signal from metadata (URL domains, sending infrastructure). Importantly, the campaign-cluster subgroup analysis partially addresses this, but it is listed as a reporting item rather than a gating condition on the main result. If most of the transformer's advantage over the baseline concentrates in the 'previously seen campaign' subgroup rather than 'novel sender/campaign' subgroup, the core claim about generalization is much weaker than the headline metric suggests.

What would constitute evidence: The novel-sender and novel-campaign subgroup metrics should be the primary claim, not a secondary reporting item. If average precision in the novel-campaign stratum is substantially lower than in the recurring-campaign stratum, the methodology is measuring campaign memorization, not generalizable detection. The paper should pre-commit to which subgroup result falsifies the generalization claim.

**Issue 3: The transformer's text pathway and the GBT baseline do not share the same information floor, so the comparison conflates modality value with information advantage.**

The specific claim: the comparison tests the value of raw-text representation rather than tuning asymmetry.

Why this might be wrong: The methodology notes that tuning effort is equalized. However, the transformer fusion model receives raw subject and body text, MIME structure, headers, authentication results, URLs, attachment metadata, and reputation logs. The GBT baseline receives 'engineered metadata and reputation features only.' If URL text, subject line tokens, or body n-grams are not in the GBT feature set, the comparison conflates 'text representation adds value' with 'the model that receives more information sources performs better.' A GBT model with bag-of-words or TF-IDF features over subject and body text is the missing intermediate baseline. Without it, you cannot attribute the performance delta to the transformer's representational capacity rather than to simple information asymmetry.

What would constitute evidence: Add a GBT + TF-IDF baseline. If the transformer meaningfully outperforms GBT + TF-IDF at comparable recall operating points, the claim about deep text representation is supported. If GBT + TF-IDF already closes most of the gap over metadata-only GBT, the transformer's marginal value is primarily its representation, not the decision to include text at all, and the ablation needs to be restructured accordingly.

**Issue 4: Average precision on an imbalanced security dataset is sensitive to label-source composition, and the three label sources (analyst triage, user reports, incident-response) likely have different recall properties over the test month.**

The specific claim: average precision is the appropriate primary metric for this environment.

Why this might be wrong: Average precision weights each threshold point by the fraction of positives recovered at that threshold. In a multi-source positive label construction, if month 6 has a different ratio of analyst-triaged vs. user-reported vs. IR-confirmed positives than months 1–4 (which is plausible — IR confirmations accumulate over time, user-report rates vary by campaign visibility), the effective positive class difficulty distribution differs between train and test. A campaign that generates many user reports is highly visible and likely easy; an IR-only confirmed campaign is hard. If month 6's positives are disproportionately IR-confirmed (because user-visible campaigns are over-represented in earlier months), test average precision will understate generalization quality for the easy cases and the model may appear to underperform due to composition shift rather than true generalization failure. Conversely, if month 6 has more user-report positives, average precision is inflated.

What would constitute evidence: Report average precision stratified by label source in both validation and test months, and report the label-source composition of each month. If the composition shifts substantially, interpret aggregate average precision with caution and treat the label-source stratum results as the primary generalization evidence.
defender_raw:
## Pass 1 — Analysis of Anticipated Criticisms

### 1. Label Construction and Quality

**What a critic would claim:** The positive class is assembled from heterogeneous sources (analyst triage, user reports, IR confirmations), each with different false-positive rates and coverage biases. Analyst triage may miss low-volume spear-phishing; user reports are biased toward visually obvious attacks. The 21-day negative window is arbitrary and too short — some compromise chains surface weeks or months later.

**Assessment:** Partially valid. The design explicitly drops disputed and unresolved labels, which is the right call — forcing ambiguous items into a noisy class is worse than discarding them. The multi-source positive construction actually increases recall of the positive class relative to any single source alone, and the requirement for later incident linkage as a disqualifier for negatives provides a meaningful backstop. The 21-day window is a real limitation: slow-burn BEC campaigns or sleeper implants seeded via email could produce false negatives in the training set. However, this is an operationally common constraint, and it is correctly scoped in the stated limitation ('it will not by itself prove superiority for every deployment policy'). The heterogeneity of label sources is a genuine concern for calibration — the model may learn the artifacts of which-analyst-flagged-it rather than which-email-is-malicious. The subgroup analysis by label-source strata is the right mitigation. **Remaining genuine uncertainty:** Whether the label-source confound is large enough to distort learned representations, particularly if analyst triage and IR confirmations cluster differently by time period (a plausible scenario during incident spikes).

### 2. Chronological Split and Temporal Leakage

**What a critic would claim:** Campaign recurrence across splits means that some test-month campaigns also appear in training, making the model partially memorize campaign fingerprints rather than generalize. The 4:1:1 month split may produce a small validation set that leads to noisy model selection.

**Assessment:** The design explicitly preserves cross-month recurrence because that reflects deployment reality — this is the correct decision. If a campaign runs for three months, the model should be rewarded for recognizing it in the fourth month. Leakage concern is addressed by deduplicating near-identical parser duplicates *within* splits, not across them, which is exactly right. The harder question is whether campaign cluster overlap across train and test months inflates test-set metrics. The proposed bootstrap intervals at the campaign-cluster level and the 'novel senders vs. previously seen senders' subgroup analysis are the correct diagnostic tools. If performance collapses on novel senders, the leakage concern is validated empirically. The 1-month validation set is indeed small for stable hyperparameter selection in an enterprise context where positive base rates may be on the order of 0.1–1%; average precision estimates over one month could have high variance. This is a real calibration risk but is inherent to the operational constraint of using only six months of data. **Remaining genuine uncertainty:** Whether one month of validation is sufficient for stable model selection, particularly for the transformer's many hyperparameters.

### 3. Model Architecture and Preprocessing Leakage

**What a critic would claim:** Transformer fine-tuning on raw email text could pick up temporal artifacts — campaign-specific vocabulary, sender names, domain strings — that inflate training-period metrics and degrade on genuinely novel threats. The fusion of structured features with the transformer output via a small classification head may be undertrained if the head doesn't receive enough gradient signal relative to the large pretrained backbone.

**Assessment:** The temporal artifact concern is real but is the same leakage concern addressed above — the campaign-cluster analysis will expose it. The preprocessing leakage rules are explicitly and correctly stated: learned preprocessing fit on training only, frozen before applying to validation/test. Rule-based parsing is fixed globally. Feature exclusion of post-receipt dispositions (quarantine status, click outcomes) is explicit. The classification head training concern is a legitimate engineering risk but is not a methodological flaw — it is a tuning problem that the validation month is designed to catch. **Remaining genuine uncertainty:** Whether the transformer backbone requires domain-adaptive pretraining on email corpora to represent MIME-mangled text effectively; a generic language model may not tokenize obfuscated URLs or base64-encoded payloads well.

### 4. Baseline Fairness

**What a critic would claim:** Even with 'comparable tuning effort,' GBT and transformer models have qualitatively different hyperparameter spaces. 'Comparable effort' is not a well-defined quantity and may still favor one architecture.

**Assessment:** This is a valid but manageable concern. The statement that the baseline receives 'comparable tuning effort on the validation month' is intentionally matched to the same selection criterion (average precision on month 5) and the same held-out test. The point of the baseline is not to establish a definitive GBT vs. transformer comparison under optimal conditions for both — it is to test whether raw-text representation adds value over the features that operational stacks already use. This is a well-scoped research question. The ablation removing the text pathway from the transformer provides an internal control that is more rigorous than the cross-architecture comparison alone. **Remaining genuine uncertainty:** Whether the GBT baseline uses the same reputation and authentication features that the transformer fusion head also consumes — if yes, the ablation and baseline are aligned; if the GBT has access to a richer feature set, the comparison is unfair in the other direction.

### 5. Metric Choice

**What a critic would claim:** Average precision as the primary metric can be unstable with very small positive counts over one validation month. AUROC is more stable but less operationally relevant. The 'precision at fixed recall' and 'recall at high-precision operating points' need specific threshold values stated in advance or they are post-hoc.

**Assessment:** Average precision is the correct primary metric for a ranked-stream, low-prevalence security task — the operational framing (review burden, concentration of positives at top of ranking) is precisely what average precision measures. The instability concern is real for small positive counts; this is why campaign-cluster bootstrap intervals are specified. The secondary metrics concern about threshold pre-specification is valid — if operating-point thresholds are selected after seeing test results, those numbers are not confirmatory. The methodology should pre-register the precision/recall operating points used for secondary reporting. This is a gap in the current specification. **Remaining genuine uncertainty:** Whether the positive count in month 6 is large enough for stable average-precision estimates; this depends on the enterprise's threat volume, which is not stated.

### 6. Generalizability Claims

**What a critic would claim:** Six months from one enterprise gateway cannot support claims about the method's generalizability to other organizations, email architectures, or threat actor populations.

**Assessment:** The methodology explicitly and correctly scopes this: 'This experiment can show whether the multimodal transformer generalizes better on a future month of this organization's mail stream; it will not by itself prove superiority for every deployment policy or every enterprise environment.' This is honest and appropriate. No defense needed.

### 7. Class Imbalance Handling

**What a critic would claim:** The methodology does not explicitly address class imbalance, which in enterprise mail is typically severe (1:1000 or worse). Without explicit handling, the model may optimize toward trivial negative prediction.

**Assessment:** This is a real gap. Average precision as the primary metric is robust to imbalance in evaluation, but training loss function choice (cross-entropy without weighting vs. focal loss vs. resampling) is not specified. For the transformer especially, standard cross-entropy on severely imbalanced data is known to produce poorly calibrated probability outputs. The calibration diagnostics mentioned in secondary reporting would catch this post-hoc, but it should be addressed in training design. This is a genuine methodological gap, not a fatal flaw, but it should be flagged.

### 8. Overall Soundness Assessment

The methodology is substantially well-designed. The temporal split is correct, leakage controls are explicit and correct, the baseline is appropriately scoped, the primary metric matches the operational use case, and the stated limitations are honest. Two genuine gaps exist: (a) the operating-point thresholds for secondary precision/recall metrics are not pre-specified, creating a post-hoc reporting risk, and (b) class imbalance handling in training is unspecified. Neither flaw is fatal to the methodology's ability to produce interpretable and valid results for its stated scope. The campaign-cluster bootstrap and subgroup analyses are appropriate diagnostics for the main confounds. The primary question — whether the multimodal transformer outperforms a structured-feature GBT on a held-out future month — can be answered validly by this design.

## Pass 2 — Verdict Selection

The two identified gaps (operating-point pre-specification and imbalance handling) are genuine but scoped. They do not undermine the primary metric (average precision), the temporal validity of the split, or the interpretability of the held-out test result. The core methodology is sound for its stated scope. The verdict is defense_wins, with the honest caveat that the two gaps should be addressed in the implementation specification.
all_issues_raised: The 21-day negative labeling window is applied asymmetrically: training negatives have had longer retrospective confirmation time than test-month negatives, potentially contaminating the test negative class with unconfirmed future positives and understating false-negative rate on month 6.

Cross-month campaign template recurrence means the transformer may be rewarded for campaign memorization rather than generalizable detection; the novel-campaign subgroup is listed as secondary reporting rather than the primary generalization claim, which obscures this confound.

The transformer fusion model receives raw text while the GBT baseline receives only engineered metadata, conflating modality value with information advantage; a GBT + TF-IDF intermediate baseline is absent, making it impossible to attribute the performance delta to representation quality rather than information asymmetry.

The three positive label sources (analyst triage, user reports, IR confirmation) have different recall properties and likely different month-to-month composition, making aggregate average precision sensitive to label-source distribution shift between training and test months rather than true generalization.
all_issues_adjudicated: The 21-day negative labeling window creates an asymmetric retrospective confirmation advantage for training negatives versus test-month negatives, and the defender did not propose a symmetric sensitivity analysis or otherwise bound this effect — the contamination risk in month 6's negative class remains an open empirical question.

The novel-campaign subgroup is listed as secondary reporting rather than a gating condition on the generalization claim, and the defender's response endorses this framing rather than committing to pre-specified falsification criteria for the memorization-versus-generalization question — this structural gap survives.

The GBT baseline receives only engineered metadata while the transformer receives raw text, and the defender did not propose adding a GBT+TF-IDF intermediate baseline, conceding the comparison conflates information advantage with representational advantage without resolving it.

The three positive label sources have different recall properties and plausibly different month-to-month composition; the defender acknowledged this as a 'remaining genuine uncertainty' affecting learned representations without proposing a pre-specified compositional stability check beyond post-hoc stratified reporting.
